name: Big Cottonwood Canyon
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding in the Wasatch Range near Brighton. Find quartz, feldspar, and mica in alpine meadows and rocky outcrops along Big Cottonwood Canyon.
description:
Big Cottonwood Canyon offers excellent rockhounding opportunities in the scenic Wasatch Range, just east of Salt Lake City. This public BLM land provides easy access to alpine terrain where visitors can collect a variety of minerals typical of Utah's granitic mountains. The canyon sits at elevation above 8000 feet, offering cooler temperatures and stunning views while you search for specimens.

The geology here features Precambrian and Paleozoic granitic intrusions with abundant pegmatite deposits. These igneous formations weathered over millions of years to expose quartz veins, feldspar crystals, and mica sheets in the rocky outcrops and canyon walls. The area saw historical mining activity for various minerals, making it a proven collecting location for enthusiasts.

Best collecting occurs along the canyon floor near creeks and washes where water erosion has naturally exposed fresh mineral faces. Rocky hillsides adjacent to the main canyon road also yield good specimens. The terrain is relatively open with scattered conifers and exposed granite, making it easy to spot promising collecting areas.

As BLM land, Big Cottonwood Canyon welcomes recreational mineral collectors under standard public-use rules. The site is beginner-friendly and family-accessible, though the high elevation and rocky terrain require reasonable physical fitness. Dogs are welcome on leash. The canyon road provides good vehicle access to multiple collecting zones throughout its length.
history: Big Cottonwood Canyon lies within the Wasatch Range, formed by Precambrian and Paleozoic granitic intrusions that crystallized during ancient mountain-building events. Pegmatite zones within these granites created ideal conditions for the formation of quartz, feldspar, and mica crystals that rockhounders seek today.
tips: Search the creek beds and wash areas where flowing water has naturally exposed fresh mineral faces and stripped away weathered material. Use a hand lens to identify feldspar crystal faces and mica sheets; clear quartz pieces are easy to spot against the darker granite. Bring sturdy hiking boots with good ankle support for the uneven rocky terrain, and work the hillside outcrops where granite has fractured naturally - these often contain the best specimens.
terrain_notes: Big Cottonwood Canyon terrain ranges from 8000 to 9000+ feet elevation with steep canyon walls and a scenic creek running through the bottom. The landscape features exposed granite outcrops, rocky slopes, and scattered coniferous forest (spruce and fir). Most collecting is within 0.5 to 2 miles of the parking/pullout areas. Footing is rocky and uneven with loose stone typical of mountain terrain. Walking surfaces range from canyon floor creek beds to steep hillsides.
written_directions: From Brighton, head east on Big Cottonwood Canyon Road (Utah State Road 190). This scenic highway climbs into the canyon for approximately 13 miles toward Brighton Ski Resort. Multiple pullouts and parking areas are located along the canyon road at approximately 1-mile intervals. Popular collecting areas are near mile 7 to mile 11. Park at any established pullout on either side of the road and walk upslope into exposed rocky areas or down to the creek. No permit required; personal use collection only.
road_conditions: Paved canyon road in good condition year-round. Narrow in places with hairpin turns. Winter snow and ice possible November-May. Summer access excellent.
parking_notes: Multiple unsigned pullouts along Big Cottonwood Canyon Road. Capacity varies from 2-4 vehicles at small pullouts to 8-12 at wider turnouts. No formal parking lot; pull well off road. Popular areas may be full on weekends.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Collect specimens only from public BLM land, not private holdings. Pack out all waste. Leave outcrops undisturbed for others.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No heavy equipment or blasting
Remove only loose material and specimens
Leave area as you found it
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Brighton has no services. Nearest gas, food, and lodging in Salt Lake City, approximately 25 miles west. Summer mountain services available at Brighton Ski Resort area.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain for a mountain location. Rocky hillsides and creek areas are accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. No wheelchair access. Uneven footing and loose rock common. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Elevation (8000+ ft) may affect visitors unaccustomed to altitude. Short walks (under 1 mile round trip) possible from pullouts.

faq.question: What gems can I find rockhounding at Big Cottonwood Canyon near Brighton?
faq.answer: Big Cottonwood Canyon is known for quartz crystals, feldspar, mica sheets, and topaz specimens. The best finds come from granite outcrops and pegmatite zones exposed along the canyon walls and hillsides. Water-worn specimens are common in the creek beds.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Big Cottonwood Canyon in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Big Cottonwood Canyon is public BLM land where recreational mineral collection is allowed without a permit under FLPMA regulations. Just follow the personal use and 25 lb/day limit rules.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Big Cottonwood Canyon?
faq.answer: June through September is ideal. The canyon sits at 8000+ feet elevation, so winter brings snow and ice. Summer months offer clear access to pullouts and hiking trails. Spring and fall are possible but weather is unpredictable at this elevation.

faq.question: How far is Big Cottonwood Canyon from Salt Lake City and can I drive there in a regular car?
faq.answer: Big Cottonwood Canyon is approximately 25 miles northeast of Salt Lake City. Yes, a regular passenger vehicle can access the canyon road. The paved Big Cottonwood Canyon Road is in good condition, though it winds through steep terrain with hairpin turns. Multiple pullouts for rockhounding are accessible from this main road.

faq.question: Is Big Cottonwood Canyon beginner-friendly for family rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, Big Cottonwood Canyon is excellent for beginners and families. The site is easy-rated with short walks available from roadside pullouts. Kids age 6+ can participate. No special skills are needed to collect specimens from the exposed rocky areas and creek beds. Dogs are welcome, making it family-friendly.

faq.question: What tools do I need to rockhound Big Cottonwood Canyon for quartz and feldspar?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for the rocky terrain, a small backpack, collecting bag, and hand lens for identifying specimens. A rock hammer is helpful for testing specimens but not required since much loose material is available. Bring water and sun protection for the high alpine elevation.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Big Cottonwood Canyon near Salt Lake City. Free BLM rockhounding, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit.
